name: Academic Writing
description: An elective course developing essential academic writing skills including thesis construction, evidence integration, citation, and discipline-specific writing for college preparation.

modules.title: What is Academic Writing
modules.objectives: Define academic writing and distinguish it from other writing modes; identify the key characteristics of effective academic prose.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 1: The Nature of Academic Writing
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Academic Writing
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What is Academic Writing? Key Features Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Academic Style and Tone Practice
modules.assignment: Find three different texts (a tweet, a news article, and an academic paragraph) on the same topic. Write a 300-word comparison identifying how the language, structure, and evidence use differ across the three, explaining which characteristics make each appropriate for its context.

modules.title: Reading Academic Texts
modules.objectives: Apply active reading strategies to academic texts including annotation, identifying thesis and evidence, and engaging with complex arguments.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 2: Reading Academic Texts
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Reading Academic Texts Effectively
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Active Reading Strategies for Academic Texts
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Reading and Research Practice
modules.assignment: Read a provided academic article and produce a fully annotated copy: mark the thesis, underline key evidence, circle transitional phrases, and bracket any terms requiring definition. Submit annotations plus a 200-word reading response identifying the article's central argument and one question it raises.

modules.title: Critical Thinking
modules.objectives: Apply critical thinking skills to evaluate arguments, identify assumptions, assess evidence quality, and develop independent analytical reasoning.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 3: Critical Thinking
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Critical Thinking Skills for Academic Success
modules.assignment: Read two short articles arguing opposite positions on the same issue. Write a 400-word analysis identifying the key assumptions each author makes, which argument is better supported by evidence, and what additional evidence would strengthen the weaker argument.

modules.title: Thesis & Argument
modules.objectives: Construct a strong, specific, and debatable thesis statement; understand the relationship between thesis, topic sentences, and supporting evidence.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 4: Developing a Thesis
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Developing a Strong Thesis
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Strong Thesis Statement
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Thesis and Argument Practice
modules.assignment: Write five practice thesis statements on different provided topics: for each, identify whether it is specific, debatable, and arguable, then revise each until it meets all three criteria. Choose the strongest and write a supporting paragraph outline.

modules.title: Body Paragraph Structure
modules.objectives: Construct body paragraphs with clear topic sentences, integrated evidence, and analytical commentary using the PEEL or similar framework.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 5: Paragraph Structure
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Writing Effective Body Paragraphs
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Body Paragraph Structure: PEEL Method
modules.assignment: Write three complete body paragraphs for a provided thesis: each paragraph must have a topic sentence clearly connected to the thesis, at least one piece of evidence with a signal phrase, and at least three sentences of analysis explaining how the evidence supports the thesis.

modules.title: Evidence & Quotation
modules.objectives: Select and integrate quotations and paraphrases effectively; apply the quotation sandwich method to analyze evidence rather than letting it speak for itself.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 6: Evidence and Quotation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Use Quotes in Academic Writing
modules.assignment: Select five quotations from a provided text. For each quotation, write a complete 'quotation sandwich': an introducing sentence with signal phrase, the quotation with citation, and at least two sentences of analysis connecting the quotation to a thesis claim.

modules.title: Paraphrase & Summary
modules.objectives: Accurately paraphrase and summarize source material while maintaining the original meaning; distinguish between acceptable paraphrase and plagiarism.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 7: Paraphrase and Summary
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Paraphrase Correctly: Academic Writing
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Paraphrase and Citation Practice
modules.assignment: Choose three passages from a provided academic text. For each: (1) write an accurate paraphrase that fully restates the idea in new words and sentence structure, (2) write a one-sentence summary of the same passage, and (3) add an appropriate in-text citation for each.

modules.title: Citation Styles (MLA/APA)
modules.objectives: Format citations and reference lists in both MLA and APA style; understand when each style is used and the principles behind documentation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 12: Citation and Documentation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — MLA vs APA: Key Differences Explained
modules.assignment: Take the same six sources (two books, two journal articles, two websites) and create correctly formatted citations in both MLA and APA styles. Write a one-paragraph reflection explaining three key structural differences between the two formats.

modules.title: Research Process
modules.objectives: Navigate the research process from question development through source evaluation and note-taking; conduct database searches using Boolean operators.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 12: Research Planning
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Research Process: Step by Step
modules.assignment: Develop a researchable question on a topic assigned in class. Document your research process: list your search terms and Boolean operators used, record the five most relevant sources found with brief notes on each, and write a 200-word reflection on how you refined your search.

modules.title: Literature Reviews
modules.objectives: Understand the structure and purpose of a literature review; synthesize multiple sources into a coherent overview of existing research on a topic.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 12: Literature Review
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Literature Review
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Synthesis and Review Practice
modules.assignment: Write a 400-word mini literature review on a provided topic using four supplied sources. Organize by theme (not source by source), demonstrate synthesis by showing where sources agree and disagree, and end with a gap in the research your own paper could address.

modules.title: Writing Abstracts
modules.objectives: Write a concise, informative abstract that summarizes a paper's purpose, methodology, findings, and significance in 150-250 words.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — How to Write an Abstract
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Perfect Abstract
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Academic Writing Formats Practice
modules.assignment: Write an abstract for each of three provided academic papers (one from the humanities, one from social science, one from natural science). Each abstract must be 150-250 words and include the paper's purpose, key methods or approach, main findings, and significance.

modules.title: Revision & Peer Review
modules.objectives: Apply structured revision strategies and provide constructive peer feedback that improves academic writing at the content, organization, and sentence levels.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 9: Revision and Peer Review
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Revision and Peer Review
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Give Effective Peer Review Feedback
modules.assignment: Exchange your Module 10 literature review with a classmate. Use the provided peer review rubric to evaluate their argument, evidence integration, source synthesis, and citation formatting. Write 300 words of specific, actionable feedback, then revise your own paper based on the feedback you receive.

modules.title: Academic Integrity & Plagiarism
modules.objectives: Understand academic integrity policies; recognize different forms of plagiarism including patchwriting, self-plagiarism, and accidental plagiarism; develop ethical research habits.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 13: Academic Integrity
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Academic Integrity and Plagiarism
modules.assignment: Read five provided short passages and classify each as: acceptable paraphrase, patchwriting (a form of plagiarism), direct quotation (cited), accidental plagiarism, or correct summary. Write a one-sentence explanation for each classification and rewrite any plagiarized examples correctly.

modules.title: Discipline-Specific Writing
modules.objectives: Identify how writing conventions differ across academic disciplines (humanities, social sciences, natural sciences); adapt academic writing to discipline-specific expectations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Writing Guide — Chapter 14: Writing in the Disciplines
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Writing Across the Disciplines
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Academic Writing Across Different Fields
modules.assignment: Write three 200-word pieces on the same research finding, each written in the style of a different discipline: (1) a humanities analysis, (2) a social science report section, and (3) a natural science abstract. Annotate each explaining the key disciplinary conventions you followed.

quizQuestions.question: Which characteristic best distinguishes academic writing from personal writing?
options: Academic writing is always longer.
Academic writing uses formal language, evidence-based reasoning, and precise, objective claims.
Academic writing avoids using any sources.
Academic writing is only written in third person.
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic writing is characterized by formal register, evidence-based argument, precision, objectivity, and proper citation—unlike personal writing, which is more subjective.

quizQuestions.question: Academic writing is considered 'formal' because it:
options: Uses contractions freely
Avoids slang, contractions, and casual expressions in favor of precise, professional language
Always discusses formal events
Requires exactly five paragraphs
quizQuestions.explanation: Formal academic writing avoids contractions (use 'do not' not 'don't'), slang, and colloquial expressions, using precise professional vocabulary instead.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a key feature of academic writing?
options: Expressing only personal opinions without evidence
Making claims supported by credible evidence and analysis
Writing for entertainment rather than knowledge
Using informal conversational language
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic writing grounds claims in credible evidence and demonstrates reasoning through analysis—not merely stating opinions.

quizQuestions.question: Academic writing typically avoids first person ('I') in which context?
options: All academic writing always avoids first person completely
When objectivity and evidence-based reasoning are expected, rather than personal reflection
Only in science papers, never in humanities
In the introduction only
quizQuestions.explanation: While some disciplines permit first person, academic writing contexts requiring objectivity—research reports, scientific papers—often expect third-person, evidence-focused prose.

quizQuestions.question: The intended audience for most academic writing is:
options: The general public with no specialized knowledge
A community of educated readers knowledgeable in the relevant field
Children learning to read
Only the teacher or professor who assigned the work
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic writing addresses a community of educated readers with background knowledge in the field, which shapes vocabulary, assumptions, and evidence requirements.

quizQuestions.question: Annotation in active reading means:
options: Reading quickly without stopping to make notes
Writing notes, questions, and observations in the margins of a text as you read
Copying out important sentences verbatim
Summarizing the text after finishing it
quizQuestions.explanation: Annotation is active engagement with the text—marking key ideas, asking questions, identifying the thesis and evidence—as you read rather than passively processing.

quizQuestions.question: When reading an academic text, identifying the thesis is important because:
options: The thesis tells you the length of the paper
The thesis states the central argument that organizes and motivates the entire text
The thesis always appears in the conclusion
The thesis is the least important part of an academic text
quizQuestions.explanation: The thesis is the central claim of an academic text; identifying it allows you to understand how all other parts of the text relate to and support that central argument.

quizQuestions.question: A reading response is most useful when it:
options: Simply summarizes what you read without personal engagement
Identifies the author's argument and engages critically with questions it raises for you
Agrees with everything the author argues
Is as short as possible
quizQuestions.explanation: A reading response demonstrates active engagement—identifying the argument, asking critical questions, and connecting ideas to your own understanding.

quizQuestions.question: When reading a complex academic argument, which strategy is most helpful?
options: Reading as quickly as possible without stopping
Breaking the text into sections, summarizing each in one sentence, then identifying how sections connect
Skipping sections that seem difficult
Reading only the introduction and conclusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Breaking complex academic texts into sections and summarizing each helps readers build understanding incrementally and see how the argument develops.

quizQuestions.question: Identifying transitional phrases in an academic text helps readers:
options: Count the number of paragraphs
Understand how arguments are connected and how one idea leads to the next
Find the bibliography more quickly
Identify which sections to skip
quizQuestions.explanation: Transitional phrases signal logical relationships between ideas (contrast, addition, causation), helping readers follow the argument's structure.

quizQuestions.question: Critical thinking in academic writing requires the ability to:
options: Accept all published arguments without questioning them
Evaluate the validity of arguments, assess evidence quality, and identify assumptions
Memorize all facts presented in sources
Use the most technical vocabulary possible
quizQuestions.explanation: Critical thinking involves actively questioning and evaluating—not simply accepting—arguments, evidence, and assumptions presented in academic texts.

quizQuestions.question: An assumption in an argument is:
options: A piece of evidence that is explicitly stated
An unstated belief or premise that the argument takes for granted without proving
The thesis statement of the argument
A direct quotation from a source
quizQuestions.explanation: Assumptions are unstated premises—beliefs the arguer takes for granted and does not explicitly justify, which may or may not be valid.

quizQuestions.question: Evaluating evidence quality requires asking:
options: Only how recent the evidence is
Whether the evidence is credible, relevant to the claim, and sufficient to support the conclusion
Whether the evidence appears impressive
Only whether the evidence is from a primary source
quizQuestions.explanation: Quality evidence evaluation checks credibility (is the source reliable?), relevance (does it directly support the claim?), and sufficiency (is there enough evidence?).

quizQuestions.question: Identifying the strongest counterargument to your thesis is valuable because:
options: It shows you have not thought carefully about your position
It allows you to address and refute opposition, strengthening your own argument
It means you should abandon your thesis
Counterarguments should always be avoided in academic writing
quizQuestions.explanation: Engaging with counterarguments demonstrates intellectual rigor and allows you to refute them, which actually strengthens your position.

quizQuestions.question: A well-supported academic claim is one that:
options: Is stated confidently and with passion
Is backed by credible evidence and connected to that evidence through clear reasoning
Is universally agreed upon by all scholars
Uses the most advanced vocabulary in the discipline
quizQuestions.explanation: A well-supported claim has credible evidence behind it and a clear logical connection showing how that evidence proves the claim.

quizQuestions.question: A thesis statement is 'debatable' when:
options: Everyone agrees with it as an obvious fact
Reasonable people could argue the opposite position and it requires evidence to support
It contains many complex academic words
It is stated in the first sentence of the essay
quizQuestions.explanation: A debatable thesis takes a position that reasonable people could disagree with, requiring evidence and argument to support rather than being self-evidently true.

quizQuestions.question: Which is the strongest thesis statement?
options: Social media exists and people use it a lot.
This essay will discuss the effects of social media.
Social media is bad for teenagers.
Excessive social media use correlates with increased anxiety in adolescents by limiting face-to-face interaction and creating unrealistic social comparisons.
quizQuestions.explanation: The strongest thesis is specific, debatable, and identifies the 'how' and 'why'—it previews the evidence and reasoning that will follow.

quizQuestions.question: Topic sentences in body paragraphs should:
options: Introduce a completely new topic unrelated to the thesis
State the main point of the paragraph and connect it clearly to the thesis
Be a quotation from a source
Always start with 'Furthermore'
quizQuestions.explanation: A topic sentence states the paragraph's main point and connects it to the thesis, creating coherence between body paragraphs and the central argument.

quizQuestions.question: A thesis that says 'There are pros and cons to social media' is weak because:
options: It is too long
It is too specific and narrow
It fails to take a position—it simply acknowledges that different views exist
It contains informal language
quizQuestions.explanation: A 'pros and cons' thesis avoids taking a debatable position; a strong thesis makes a specific claim that the essay will argue and prove.

quizQuestions.question: The relationship between a thesis and body paragraph topic sentences should be:
options: Unrelated—each paragraph should be independent
Each topic sentence should directly support and develop one aspect of the thesis
Topic sentences should repeat the thesis word for word
Topic sentences should contradict the thesis to show balance
quizQuestions.explanation: Body paragraph topic sentences develop the thesis—each one specifies one supporting point that collectively builds the overall argument.

quizQuestions.question: In a PEEL paragraph, what does the 'L' (Link) accomplish?
options: Lists additional evidence
Connects the paragraph's point back to the thesis and transitions to the next paragraph
Lists all sources used in the paragraph
Introduces a counterargument
quizQuestions.explanation: The Link (L) in PEEL closes the paragraph by tying the point back to the thesis and providing a smooth transition, maintaining essay coherence.

quizQuestions.question: Analysis in a body paragraph should explain:
options: What the source says, without any original interpretation
How and why the evidence supports the paragraph's claim and ultimately the thesis
A summary of all sources on the topic
The biography of the author being cited
quizQuestions.explanation: Analysis is the writer's original interpretation—explaining the significance of the evidence and the logical connection between the evidence and the claim.

quizQuestions.question: A body paragraph that consists mainly of quotations with little analysis is problematic because:
options: It contains too much evidence
It lacks the writer's own intellectual contribution—the voice and reasoning that make an academic argument original
It uses too many sources
Quotations are never appropriate in body paragraphs
quizQuestions.explanation: Over-quoting without analysis produces a paragraph that borrows others' ideas without adding original thinking—the hallmark of weak academic writing.

quizQuestions.question: The 'topic sentence' test: if you can remove a paragraph from an essay and the thesis is unchanged, this indicates:
options: The paragraph is strong and independent
The paragraph may lack a clear connection to the thesis and should be revised or removed
The thesis is too specific
The paragraph needs more evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Every body paragraph should be necessary to the thesis—if removing it changes nothing, the paragraph likely lacks a clear, direct connection to the central argument.

quizQuestions.question: The minimum sentence structure for an effective body paragraph is:
options: Topic sentence only
Topic sentence, one piece of evidence, one sentence of analysis
Topic sentence, evidence, three or more sentences of analysis, and a closing link to the thesis
A long quotation followed by a topic sentence
quizQuestions.explanation: A robust body paragraph requires a topic sentence, integrated evidence, substantial analysis (minimum 2-3 sentences), and a closing link—less produces underdevelopment.

quizQuestions.question: The 'quotation sandwich' method requires:
options: Using only bread-shaped formatting in your essay
Introducing a quotation, providing it, and then analyzing it—never letting a quotation stand alone
Using exactly three quotations per paragraph
Placing all quotations at the beginning of paragraphs
quizQuestions.explanation: The quotation sandwich: context/signal phrase + quotation + analysis. No quotation should appear without both an introduction and follow-up analysis.

quizQuestions.question: A signal phrase in academic writing serves to:
options: Replace the need for a citation
Introduce a quotation or paraphrase by naming the author and providing context
Signal the end of the essay
Indicate that a new paragraph is beginning
quizQuestions.explanation: Signal phrases attribute borrowed material to its source and provide context before the quotation or paraphrase, smoothly integrating evidence into the argument.

quizQuestions.question: Selecting the best quotation for a body paragraph means choosing:
options: The longest quotation available
A specific passage that provides the most precise, relevant evidence for the paragraph's claim
Any quotation from the source, regardless of relevance
The most difficult to understand passage
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective quotation selection requires finding the specific passage that provides the most direct, relevant support for the paragraph's particular analytical claim.

quizQuestions.question: Brackets [ ] in a quotation indicate:
options: Words that are particularly important
That the writer added or changed words to make the quotation grammatically fit the surrounding sentence
That the quotation is approximate, not exact
That the quotation comes from a secondary source
quizQuestions.explanation: Brackets indicate that the writer has modified the original text—usually adding a word for clarity or changing a pronoun—without changing the meaning.

quizQuestions.question: When should a writer choose to paraphrase rather than quote directly?
options: Never—direct quotation is always better
When the author's exact words are not as important as the idea, or when the original is too complex for smooth integration
When they cannot find the exact page number for the citation
Only when writing in APA style
quizQuestions.explanation: Paraphrase is preferable when the idea matters more than the exact wording, when the original is complex, or when direct quotation would disrupt the essay's flow.

quizQuestions.question: Proper paraphrasing requires:
options: Changing only a few words in the original sentence
Completely restating the idea in your own words and sentence structure, then citing the source
Copying the source verbatim and changing the font
Using the original sentence structure but different vocabulary
quizQuestions.explanation: True paraphrase requires completely restating the idea in your own words and sentence structure—just changing a few words while keeping the original structure is patchwriting (a form of plagiarism).

quizQuestions.question: Patchwriting is a form of academic dishonesty because:
options: It uses too many sources
It makes minimal changes to the original text, creating an inaccurate appearance of original expression
It cites too many authors
It uses the wrong citation format
quizQuestions.explanation: Patchwriting (swapping a few words while keeping the original structure) falsely presents near-copied work as original paraphrase, which is a form of plagiarism.

quizQuestions.question: A summary differs from a paraphrase in that:
options: A summary uses the author's exact words
A summary condenses the main ideas of a longer passage or entire text; a paraphrase restates one specific passage
A summary does not require a citation
A paraphrase is always shorter than a summary
quizQuestions.explanation: A summary provides the main ideas of an entire text or section in condensed form; a paraphrase restates a specific passage in full, maintaining its level of detail.

quizQuestions.question: Does a paraphrase require a citation?
options: No—only direct quotations need citations
Yes—all borrowed ideas require citation, whether quoted, paraphrased, or summarized
Only if the paraphrase is longer than two sentences
Only when the source is less than five years old
quizQuestions.explanation: Any borrowed idea—whether directly quoted, paraphrased, or summarized—requires a citation. Only your own original ideas do not need attribution.

quizQuestions.question: A successful paraphrase test is:
options: Checking whether your version sounds more complex than the original
Covering the original and writing the idea entirely from memory in your own words, then comparing for accuracy
Ensuring your version is exactly the same length as the original
Checking that no word in your version appears in the original
quizQuestions.explanation: The best paraphrase technique: read the original, cover it, write the idea from memory, then check your version against the original for accuracy without copying structure.

quizQuestions.question: APA citation style is most commonly used in:
options: Literature, languages, and cultural studies
Social sciences, education, and psychology
Medical and clinical research
Legal studies and law reviews
quizQuestions.explanation: APA (American Psychological Association) style is the standard in social sciences, education, and psychology, emphasizing publication dates.

quizQuestions.question: MLA in-text citations for a direct quote from author Smith on page 45 appear as:
options: (Smith 45)
[Smith 45]
(45, Smith)
quizQuestions.explanation: MLA format: (Author's last name page number) with no comma and no 'p.' — (Smith 45).

quizQuestions.question: APA in-text citations include which elements?
options: Author's full name, title, and URL
Author's last name, year of publication, and page/paragraph number for direct quotes
Author's first name and publisher
Journal title and volume number
quizQuestions.explanation: APA in-text: (Author, Year, p. #) — e.g., (Smith, 2020, p. 45) for direct quotations; (Smith, 2020) for paraphrase.

quizQuestions.question: A hanging indent in a Works Cited/References page means:
options: The title of the page is indented
The first line of each entry is flush left, with subsequent lines indented
Every line is equally indented
Only the author's name is indented
quizQuestions.explanation: A hanging indent is the standard format for both MLA Works Cited and APA References: first line flush left, all continuation lines indented 0.5 inches.

quizQuestions.question: The primary purpose of citation is to:
options: Make papers look longer and more scholarly
Give credit to original sources and allow readers to locate them
Demonstrate that the writer has read many books
Replace the need for original analysis
quizQuestions.explanation: Citation serves two essential purposes: crediting the original author (academic integrity) and enabling readers to locate and verify sources.

quizQuestions.question: A well-formed research question should be:
options: Answerable with a simple yes or no
Open, specific, and requiring investigation through sources to answer
Completely based on personal opinion
Identical to the essay's final thesis
quizQuestions.explanation: A good research question is open-ended, specific enough to research thoroughly, and requires genuine investigation—it guides but does not predetermine the answer.

quizQuestions.question: Boolean operator AND in database searching:
options: Broadens the search to include either term
Narrows the search to require both terms appear in results
Excludes one term from search results
Searches for exact phrase only
quizQuestions.explanation: AND narrows a search—requiring both terms to appear in results, reducing the number but increasing the relevance of results.

quizQuestions.question: An academic database such as JSTOR or Google Scholar is preferable to a general web search for research because:
options: It is faster to search
It indexes peer-reviewed and scholarly sources evaluated for quality
It contains more results
It is free to access
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic databases index peer-reviewed, scholarly sources—ensuring higher quality, credibility, and relevance than general web searches.

quizQuestions.question: Note-taking during research should include:
options: Copying all source text verbatim with no attribution
The source information, a summary/paraphrase in your own words, and your own analytical reactions
Only direct quotations from each source
Only the URL or library call number for each source
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective research notes include source attribution, your own paraphrase of key ideas, and your own analytical reactions—enabling later writing without plagiarism.

quizQuestions.question: Refining your research question after initial investigation is:
options: A sign of poor planning that should be avoided
A normal and valuable part of the research process as you discover what the literature actually covers
Only acceptable if the teacher approves each change
A sign that the original question was completely wrong
quizQuestions.explanation: Research questions naturally evolve as researchers discover what the literature actually addresses—refinement is a sign of intellectual engagement, not poor planning.

quizQuestions.question: A literature review differs from a research paper in that it primarily:
options: Presents original new data
Surveys, synthesizes, and evaluates existing research on a topic
Argues a single thesis position using one source
Summarizes one book in detail
quizQuestions.explanation: A literature review maps and synthesizes the existing body of research on a topic, identifying patterns, gaps, and debates—it does not present new primary research.

quizQuestions.question: Organizing a literature review thematically means:
options: Summarizing each source separately in alphabetical order
Grouping sources by the themes or perspectives they share, regardless of which source they come from
Organizing sources by publication date only
Listing sources in the order you found them
quizQuestions.explanation: Thematic organization groups sources by shared ideas or perspectives, creating a coherent analytical overview rather than a series of isolated summaries.

quizQuestions.question: Identifying a 'gap' in the research literature means:
options: Finding a source you could not access
Noting an aspect of the topic not yet addressed or insufficiently studied by existing research
Finding a source that contradicts others
Identifying a source published outside your research area
quizQuestions.explanation: A research gap is an area that has not been addressed or is underexplored in existing literature—identifying it justifies why new research is needed.

quizQuestions.question: Synthesis in a literature review means:
options: Summarizing each source completely before moving to the next
Weaving together multiple sources to show relationships, agreements, and disagreements
Choosing the single best source and focusing only on that
Avoiding any quotations from sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Synthesis integrates multiple sources into a coherent discussion—showing where they agree, disagree, or complement each other—rather than presenting each separately.

quizQuestions.question: A literature review should be organized around:
options: The researcher's preference for certain authors
Themes, debates, or methodological approaches in the research, not individual source summaries
The order in which you read the sources
Alphabetical order of author last names
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective literature reviews are organized by themes, patterns, or debates in the research—not by source—creating an analytical overview of the field.

quizQuestions.question: An abstract in academic writing primarily serves to:
options: Replace the introduction in a paper
Provide a concise summary of the paper's purpose, method, findings, and significance
List all sources used in the paper
Explain the writer's personal interest in the topic
quizQuestions.explanation: An abstract is a brief, standalone summary—typically 150-250 words—covering the paper's purpose, approach, key findings, and significance, allowing readers to quickly assess relevance.

quizQuestions.question: An abstract should be written:
options: Before the paper is drafted
After the paper is complete, as it summarizes the finished work
While the research is still being conducted
By someone other than the paper's author
quizQuestions.explanation: The abstract should be written last—only after the complete paper is finished can you accurately summarize its purpose, methods, findings, and significance.

quizQuestions.question: A well-written abstract does NOT include:
options: The paper's main argument or purpose
New information not in the paper
Key findings or conclusions
The research approach or methodology
quizQuestions.explanation: An abstract only summarizes what is already in the paper—it should never introduce new information, examples, or arguments not discussed in the full text.

quizQuestions.question: An abstract for a science paper typically includes:
options: Only the research question with no results
Purpose, methods, key results, and conclusions
Personal reflections on the research process
The full bibliography
quizQuestions.explanation: Scientific abstracts follow a structured format: purpose/background, methods, results, and conclusions—giving readers a complete preview of the paper.

quizQuestions.question: The significance statement in an abstract explains:
options: How the research was funded
Why the findings matter—their implications for the field or broader practice
The author's qualifications
The limitations of the study only
quizQuestions.explanation: The significance statement addresses 'so what?'—explaining why the research findings matter and how they contribute to knowledge or practice in the field.

quizQuestions.question: Effective peer review feedback is characterized by:
options: General praise without specific suggestions
Specific, actionable observations about strengths and areas for improvement grounded in the text
Only identifying grammar errors
Rewriting sections of the writer's paper
quizQuestions.explanation: Useful peer feedback is specific—identifying exact passages, explaining what works and what needs improvement, and suggesting concrete revisions.

quizQuestions.question: The most important feedback to address during revision is:
options: Spelling and grammar corrections only
Feedback on argument clarity, evidence integration, and thesis development—global issues over surface errors
Any feedback that is easiest to implement
Only positive feedback that affirms existing choices
quizQuestions.explanation: Global issues—thesis, argument, evidence—should be addressed before surface-level editing, as they fundamentally affect the essay's effectiveness.

quizQuestions.question: Using a structured rubric during peer review helps reviewers to:
options: Focus only on personal preferences
Systematically evaluate specific elements of the writing against clear criteria
Complete the review more quickly without reading carefully
Give higher scores to friends
quizQuestions.explanation: A rubric provides clear criteria for evaluation, ensuring peer feedback is systematic, fair, and covers all essential elements of academic writing.

quizQuestions.question: After receiving peer feedback, the next step is to:
options: Accept every suggestion without question
Evaluate each suggestion critically—deciding which to implement and which to reject with reasoning
Submit the original draft without changes
Only implement changes suggested by the most positive review
quizQuestions.explanation: Writers must exercise critical judgment with peer feedback—evaluating each suggestion on its merits and making informed decisions about revision.

quizQuestions.question: Self-revision before peer review is valuable because it:
options: Prevents classmates from finding errors
Allows you to address the most obvious issues yourself, making peer feedback more focused on deeper problems
Is required by all academic institutions
Eliminates the need for peer review altogether
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-revision before sharing with peers addresses obvious surface issues, freeing peer reviewers to focus on deeper structural and argumentative concerns.

quizQuestions.question: Plagiarism is defined as:
options: Using sources in your research
Presenting someone else's words, ideas, or work as your own without proper attribution
Writing about the same topic as another author
Using more than five sources in one paper
quizQuestions.explanation: Plagiarism is using another person's ideas, words, or work without giving proper credit, regardless of whether it is intentional or accidental.

quizQuestions.question: Self-plagiarism occurs when a student:
options: Copies from their own diary
Submits the same paper or substantial portions of it for multiple courses without instructor permission
Proofreads their own work
Revises a previously graded essay to improve it
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-plagiarism is reusing your own previously submitted work for a new assignment without disclosure and instructor permission.

quizQuestions.question: Accidental plagiarism occurs when:
options: A student deliberately copies from a source
A student misattributes, misquotes, or improperly paraphrases without intending to deceive
A student uses too many sources
A student forgets to write a conclusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Accidental plagiarism results from errors in note-taking, paraphrasing, or citation—not from intentional dishonesty—but is still a violation of academic integrity.

quizQuestions.question: Which practice best protects against plagiarism?
options: Using only your own ideas and no sources
Keeping careful research notes that distinguish your own ideas from source material, and citing all borrowed ideas
Only using sources that are difficult to find online
Rewriting every sentence with a thesaurus
quizQuestions.explanation: Careful, organized note-taking that distinguishes your ideas from sources, combined with proper citation of all borrowed material, is the best defense against plagiarism.

quizQuestions.question: Common knowledge does NOT require citation. Common knowledge refers to:
options: Any fact that appears in more than one source
Facts so widely known and uncontested that no one would question their origin (e.g., 'World War II ended in 1945')
Your instructor's lecture notes
All information available on Wikipedia
quizQuestions.explanation: Common knowledge is widely known, uncontested factual information that needs no attribution. Specific claims, statistics, analyses, and interpretations still require citation.

quizQuestions.question: Writing conventions differ across disciplines because:
options: Different professors have different personal preferences only
Each discipline has its own methods of inquiry, evidence standards, and ways of establishing knowledge
Academic writing is identical in all fields
Only the citation format changes between disciplines
quizQuestions.explanation: Each academic discipline has its own epistemology—methods, evidence standards, and ways of knowing—which shape the writing conventions expected in that field.

quizQuestions.question: Humanities writing (literature, history, philosophy) typically emphasizes:
options: Quantitative data and statistical analysis
Textual interpretation, close reading, and argument through analysis of primary sources
Laboratory methods and experimental results
Survey data and demographic statistics
quizQuestions.explanation: Humanities writing centers on interpretation and argument, using primary texts, historical documents, or philosophical works as evidence analyzed through close reading.

quizQuestions.question: Social science writing (psychology, sociology, economics) typically emphasizes:
options: Only subjective personal interpretation
Empirical evidence, data analysis, and reference to established theories and prior research
Poetic language and literary devices
Personal narrative without citations
quizQuestions.explanation: Social sciences rely on empirical methods—data, studies, experiments—and situate findings within established theoretical frameworks and prior research.

quizQuestions.question: Natural science writing (biology, chemistry, physics) is typically structured as:
options: A narrative story about discovery
IMRaD: Introduction, Methods, Results, and Discussion
Five literary paragraphs with no data
A personal reflection on scientific questions
quizQuestions.explanation: Scientific papers typically follow the IMRaD structure (Introduction, Methods, Results, and Discussion), reflecting the scientific method.

quizQuestions.question: Adapting your writing to a specific discipline requires:
options: Only changing the citation format
Understanding and applying the discipline's evidence standards, argumentation style, and structural conventions
Using the same approach as literary analysis in every field
Avoiding all technical vocabulary
quizQuestions.explanation: Writing in a discipline requires learning its conventions—what counts as evidence, how arguments are structured, what vocabulary is appropriate, and how knowledge is communicated.

questions.question: Which characteristic best defines academic writing?
options: Uses casual, conversational language
Formal register, evidence-based argument, and precise claims with proper citation
Always written in first person
Avoids using any outside sources
questions.explanation: Academic writing is characterized by formal language, claims supported by credible evidence, and proper citation of all sources.

questions.question: Active reading of an academic text involves:
options: Reading as quickly as possible
Annotating, identifying the thesis, and engaging critically with arguments
Reading only the abstract and conclusion
Copying important sentences verbatim
questions.explanation: Active reading involves annotation, identifying the thesis and evidence, and critically engaging with the author's argument—not passive processing.

questions.question: An assumption in an argument is:
options: A stated piece of evidence
An unstated belief the argument takes for granted
The thesis statement
A direct quotation
questions.explanation: An assumption is an unstated premise the argument depends on without explicit proof.

questions.question: Which thesis statement is the most specific and debatable?
options: Climate change is a topic people disagree about.
This essay will examine climate policy.
Climate change is bad for the environment.
Federal investment in renewable energy infrastructure would more effectively reduce carbon emissions than individual consumer choices alone.
questions.explanation: Option D is specific, debatable, and makes a clear analytical claim that requires evidence and argument to support.

questions.question: In a PEEL paragraph, analysis (E—Explain) should:
options: Restate what the quotation says in different words
Explain how and why the evidence connects to and proves the paragraph's claim
Provide additional quotations
Introduce the next paragraph's topic
questions.explanation: The Explain element is the writer's original interpretation—showing the logical connection between the evidence and the claim being made.

questions.question: A signal phrase does which of the following?
options: Indicates the end of the essay
Introduces borrowed material by naming the author and providing context
Replaces the need for an in-text citation
Lists sources alphabetically
questions.explanation: A signal phrase smoothly introduces quoted or paraphrased material, attributing it to its source before the citation.

questions.question: True paraphrasing requires the writer to:
options: Change only a few words while keeping the original sentence structure
Completely restate the idea in new words and sentence structure, then cite the source
Copy the source and change the font to differentiate it
Use the original word order with synonyms substituted throughout
questions.explanation: Proper paraphrase requires completely new words and sentence structure—just substituting synonyms while keeping the original structure is patchwriting.

questions.question: MLA format in-text citations include:
options: Author, year, and page number
Author's last name and page number only
Author's full name and the publication title
Only the page number
questions.explanation: MLA in-text citations use (Author's last name page number) — e.g., (Smith 45) — without a comma or 'p.'

questions.question: Boolean operator AND in a database search:
options: Broadens results to include either term
Narrows results by requiring both terms to appear
Excludes one term from all results
Searches for exact phrase sequences
questions.explanation: AND narrows searches—both search terms must appear in results, reducing quantity but increasing relevance.

questions.question: A literature review is organized most effectively by:
options: Summarizing each source separately in alphabetical order
Themes, debates, or methodological approaches in the research
The order in which sources were found
Publication date from oldest to newest
questions.explanation: Thematic organization synthesizes sources by shared ideas, creating an analytical map of the field rather than isolated summaries.

questions.question: An abstract is written:
options: Before the paper to plan its structure
After the paper is complete, summarizing its finished content
By a classmate who reads the draft
As a replacement for the introduction
questions.explanation: The abstract summarizes the completed paper—it can only be written accurately after the paper is finished.

questions.question: Patchwriting is a form of plagiarism because it:
options: Uses too many direct quotations
Makes minimal changes to the original text, creating a false impression of original expression
Cites sources incorrectly
Uses sources without analyzing them
questions.explanation: Patchwriting substitutes a few words while keeping the original structure—it mimics paraphrase without achieving it, which is academically dishonest.

questions.question: Humanities writing differs from social science writing primarily in its:
options: Use of longer papers
Emphasis on textual interpretation and close reading rather than empirical data and statistical methods
Avoidance of all citations
Use of APA citation format
questions.explanation: Humanities writing centers on interpretation and textual analysis; social science writing emphasizes empirical evidence, data, and theoretical frameworks.

questions.question: Self-plagiarism occurs when a student:
options: Edits their own work
Submits the same paper for multiple courses without instructor permission
Reads their own previous papers for inspiration
Reviews their previous work during revision
questions.explanation: Self-plagiarism is resubmitting your own previously graded work as new without disclosure—violating academic integrity even though the work is your own.

questions.question: A 'gap' in the research literature refers to:
options: A source you could not access
An aspect of the topic not yet adequately addressed by existing research
A discrepancy between two sources
A source published before 2000
questions.explanation: A research gap is an underexplored aspect of a topic that existing literature has not adequately addressed, justifying new research.

questions.question: IMRaD structure in natural science writing stands for:
options: Introduction, Main argument, References, and Discussion
Introduction, Methods, Results, and Discussion
Introduction, Methodology, Research, and Data
Issues, Methods, Results, and Debate
questions.explanation: IMRaD—Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion—is the standard structure for empirical scientific papers, reflecting the scientific method.

questions.question: A hanging indent format means that:
options: Every line of the citation is indented equally
The first line is flush left and all subsequent lines are indented
Only the author's name appears on the first line
The last line of each citation is indented
questions.explanation: Hanging indent: first line flush left, all continuation lines indented—used in both MLA Works Cited and APA References.

questions.question: The significance section of an abstract explains:
options: The author's academic background
Why the research findings matter and their implications
The budget for the research project
The limitations of the study only
questions.explanation: The significance or implications section explains why the research matters—what it contributes to knowledge, practice, or policy in the field.

questions.question: Effective peer review requires that feedback be:
options: General and vague to avoid hurting feelings
Specific, actionable, and grounded in the text being reviewed
Only positive observations
Written as briefly as possible
questions.explanation: Useful peer feedback identifies specific passages, explains what works and what needs improvement, and provides concrete revision suggestions.

questions.question: The quotation sandwich method requires that every quotation be:
options: Placed at the end of every paragraph
Introduced with context, provided with citation, and followed by analysis
Used without any surrounding commentary
At least three sentences long
questions.explanation: The quotation sandwich: introduce with signal phrase → provide quotation → analyze. No quotation should stand alone without both contextualization and analysis.

questions.question: Common knowledge does not require citation. Which of the following IS common knowledge?
options: The specific economic impacts of World War II calculated by a researcher
World War II ended in 1945
A historian's interpretation of why World War II began
A museum's specific exhibition about World War II
questions.explanation: Widely known, uncontested facts—'World War II ended in 1945'—are common knowledge requiring no citation. Specific claims, statistics, and interpretations always need citation.

questions.question: APA citation style is most commonly used in which fields?
options: Literature and the arts
Social and behavioral sciences, education, and psychology
Medical and legal studies
Philosophy and theology
questions.explanation: APA is the standard for social and behavioral sciences, education, and psychology, emphasizing the currency of research through publication dates.

questions.question: Synthesizing sources in a literature review means:
options: Summarizing each source completely and separately
Integrating sources to show relationships, agreements, and contradictions across the research
Selecting only one authoritative source to rely on
Avoiding direct comparison between sources
questions.explanation: Synthesis weaves sources together—showing patterns, agreements, and debates across the literature—creating an analytical overview rather than a series of summaries.

questions.question: Adapting academic writing to a specific discipline requires understanding:
options: Only which citation format to use
The discipline's evidence standards, argumentation style, and structural conventions
How long papers in that discipline typically are
Which universities specialize in that discipline
questions.explanation: Each discipline has its own epistemology—what counts as evidence, how arguments are structured, what vocabulary and methods are appropriate.

questions.question: The best defense against accidental plagiarism is:
options: Only using your own ideas with no research
Careful note-taking that distinguishes your ideas from sources, combined with citation of all borrowed material
Using a thesaurus to change every word in a paraphrase
Avoiding all direct quotations
questions.explanation: Organized research notes that clearly separate your ideas from source material, combined with careful citation practice, prevent accidental plagiarism.

questions.question: A research question should be refined during the research process when:
options: It is always inappropriate to change the original question
Initial investigation reveals the question is too broad, too narrow, or that the literature addresses a more specific angle
The instructor assigns a new topic
You run out of sources
questions.explanation: Research questions naturally evolve as researchers learn more about a topic—refinement in response to evidence is a sign of intellectual engagement.

questions.question: A topic sentence must be directly connected to the thesis because:
options: They should be the same sentence repeated
Each body paragraph must develop one aspect of the thesis—disconnected paragraphs undermine essay coherence
Topic sentences should introduce new topics unrelated to the thesis
It makes the paper appear longer
questions.explanation: Essay coherence requires that every body paragraph's topic sentence directly develops the thesis—unrelated paragraphs fragment the argument.

questions.question: Brackets [ ] in a direct quotation indicate that the writer has:
options: Marked an error in the original text
Added or changed words for grammatical clarity, without changing the meaning
Removed words from the original text
Emphasized a particularly important word
questions.explanation: Brackets indicate a writer's addition or modification to a quotation to make it grammatically fit the surrounding sentence—they preserve transparency about changes.

questions.question: An effective research note system distinguishes:
options: Sources by their color-coded covers
Your own ideas and analysis from paraphrased source material and direct quotations
Only between primary and secondary sources
Sources by their length only
questions.explanation: Effective research notes clearly separate the researcher's own ideas from paraphrased material and direct quotations—preventing accidental plagiarism during writing.

questions.question: Global revision addresses which type of issues?
options: Spelling and punctuation errors
Thesis clarity, argument structure, evidence integration, and paragraph development
Font and formatting choices
Page numbering and margins
questions.explanation: Global revision addresses big-picture issues that affect the essay's intellectual effectiveness—thesis, structure, argument, evidence—not surface-level mechanics.

questions.question: Why must a paraphrase always be cited even though it uses the writer's own words?
options: It does not need to be cited—paraphrase is free from attribution requirements
Because the idea belongs to the original author, even when expressed in different words
Only paraphrases longer than two sentences need citation
Only APA requires citation of paraphrases, not MLA
questions.explanation: Attribution is required for ideas, not just words—a paraphrase borrows the original author's intellectual contribution and must always be cited.

questions.question: The most important element of an academic thesis is that it:
options: Appears in the first sentence of the essay
Makes a specific, debatable claim that the essay will argue and prove
Uses the most advanced vocabulary possible
Is at least two sentences long
questions.explanation: The essential quality of an academic thesis is that it makes a specific, arguable claim requiring evidence and reasoning to support—not a statement of fact or vague observation.

questions.question: Social science writing typically uses which type of evidence?
options: Close textual analysis of literary works
Empirical data, research studies, and statistical analysis
Historical primary documents only
Personal anecdotes and individual experience
questions.explanation: Social sciences rely on empirical evidence—data from studies, surveys, experiments—organized within theoretical frameworks from the discipline.

questions.question: Academic integrity requires that students:
options: Use only their own ideas without any outside research
Credit all sources, submit original work, and represent their knowledge honestly
Always write without consulting notes during exams
Submit identical work independently for group assignments
questions.explanation: Academic integrity encompasses proper citation of all sources, submission of original work, and honest representation of one's own knowledge and effort.

questions.question: Writing three pieces on the same topic for different disciplines demonstrates understanding of:
options: Only vocabulary differences between fields
How each discipline's unique evidence standards, argumentation style, and structural conventions shape writing
That all academic writing is essentially the same
How to change citation formats from MLA to APA
questions.explanation: Discipline-specific writing reflects each field's epistemology—producing fundamentally different texts from the same content when adapting to different disciplinary conventions.
